title: Is the Strait of Hormuz Open for Ships?
summary: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow waterway in the Middle East that is very important for ships carrying oil and goods. Recently, Iran and the U.S. said it is open, but experts like Ian Ralby explain it is not fully open. Iran is controlling the strait by making ships ask for permission and follow special routes near its coast. This means ships cannot go freely, which affects global trade. The situation is uncertain, and it is hard to know when it will be truly open again. This article helps us understand why this matters for shipping and the world.

keywords.explanation: a narrow sea passage in the Middle East where many ships travel
a country that controls part of the Strait of Hormuz
large boats that carry things like oil across the sea
asking for and getting approval to do something
the buying and selling of goods between countries

options: No, it is controlled by Iran

questions.question: What is uncertain in the article?
options: When the strait will be fully open

background_read: The Strait of Hormuz is a key waterway between Iran and Oman, connecting the Persian Gulf to the ocean. It is vital for global trade because many oil tankers pass through it. When countries like Iran control it, it can slow down shipping and affect prices worldwide. This article talks about how even though officials say it is open, in reality, ships face restrictions, making recovery uncertain.
Article_Structure: WHO: Ian Ralby, an expert on ocean governance, and officials from Iran and the U.S.
WHAT: The Strait of Hormuz is not fully open despite claims; Iran controls it with conditions.
WHERE: The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow sea passage in the Middle East.
WHY: To explain why shipping recovery is uncertain and how it impacts global trade.

perspectives.description: Iran and U.S. officials say the strait is open, suggesting normal operations.
Ian Ralby argues it is not truly open because Iran imposes restrictions, affecting commerce.
